title: Receptacle spacing in habitable rooms
text: Electrical work should be done or verified by a licensed electrician and permitted and inspected. As reference: in habitable rooms, receptacle outlets are spaced so that no point along the floor line of any wall is more than 6 feet from a receptacle, which means receptacles at most 12 feet apart, and any wall section 2 feet or wider counts. This keeps appliances from needing extension cords. Confirm the layout and all connections with a licensed electrician and verify against NEC 210.52(A) for your adopted edition.

dimensions.value: 6 ft along the wall floor line

dimensions.note: the 6 ft rule stated as spacing

steps: Engage a licensed electrician; pull the electrical permit.
Lay out receptacles so no wall point is over 6 ft from an outlet.
Count any wall segment 2 ft or wider, including fixed panels beside doors.
Schedule the rough-in and final inspections.
common_mistakes: Leaving a wall segment over 6 ft from the nearest outlet.
Not counting short wall sections that still require a receptacle.
Doing the work without a permit or licensed electrician.

jurisdiction_notes.fl: FL adopts the NEC through the FBC; confirm the adopted NEC edition (often a cycle behind the latest).
jurisdiction_notes.nc: NC adopts the NEC as the NC Electrical Code; confirm the enforced edition with the AHJ.

title: GFCI and AFCI protection
text: Electrical work should be done or verified by a licensed electrician. As reference: GFCI protection (ground-fault, which trips on current leakage) is required at receptacles in wet or damp locations such as bathrooms, kitchens, garages, unfinished basements, outdoors, and near sinks. AFCI protection (arc-fault, which trips on dangerous arcing) is required for most branch circuits serving dwelling living areas. Provide GFCI and AFCI protection where required, confirm with a licensed electrician, and verify against NEC 210.8 and 210.12 for your adopted edition.

dimensions.value: bath, kitchen, garage, basement, outdoors, near sinks

steps: Identify which receptacles and circuits fall under GFCI and AFCI rules.
Install GFCI devices or breakers at the required locations.
Install AFCI protection on the required branch circuits.
Test each device and have the work inspected.
common_mistakes: Missing GFCI at a garage, basement, or outdoor receptacle.
No AFCI protection on bedroom and living-area circuits.
Confusing GFCI and AFCI and installing the wrong device.

jurisdiction_notes.fl: Confirm the adopted NEC edition; GFCI and AFCI scope expanded in recent cycles.
jurisdiction_notes.nc: Confirm the enforced NC Electrical Code edition; scope differs by cycle.

title: Wire size, breaker size, and box fill
text: Electrical work should be done or verified by a licensed electrician. As reference: conductor ampacity must match the overcurrent device, so 14 AWG copper is protected at 15 amps, 12 AWG at 20 amps, and 10 AWG at 30 amps under the small-conductor rule. A junction or device box has a maximum fill based on the number and size of conductors, devices, and clamps inside it; an overfilled box overheats and is unsafe. Match wire to breaker, keep boxes within their fill limit, confirm with a licensed electrician, and verify against NEC 240.4(D) and 314.16 for your adopted edition.

dimensions.note: NEC 314.16; the build-calc box_fill tool computes required volume

steps: Select conductor size for the load, then protect it at the matching breaker.
Count conductors, devices, and clamps in each box and compute required volume.
Choose a box whose volume meets or exceeds the required fill.
Have a licensed electrician verify and the work inspected.
common_mistakes: A 20 A breaker on 14 AWG wire (or any wire-breaker mismatch).
Overfilled boxes packed past the fill limit.
Back-stabbed connections instead of terminal screws on higher-load circuits.

jurisdiction_notes.fl: Confirm the adopted NEC edition through the FBC.
jurisdiction_notes.nc: Confirm the enforced NC Electrical Code edition.
